management_steps.action: Assess stability: hypotension, acute cardiac failure, chest pain, dyspnoea, altered mental status
Unstable AF: Synchronised cardioversion 150-200J (consider max dose first, max 3 attempts). Use BP-preserving analgesia/sedation (fentanyl/ketamine/midazolam)
If cardioversion fails AND no WPW: Amiodarone 150mg IV in D5W over 30min then 900mg in 200ml D5W at 8.3ml/hr. Consider MgSO4 2-4g IV over 15min
Stable AF: Determine if candidate for cardioversion (< 48hrs, new onset, no LV dysfunction, no valvular disease, no VTE history, therapeutic anticoagulation)
If candidate for cardioversion: synchronised cardioversion with analgesia/sedation. Consider anticoagulation
If NOT candidate for cardioversion: RATE CONTROL (goal HR < 110bpm)
Rate control options: Amiodarone 150mg IV then infusion OR Beta-blocker (Carvedilol 3.125mg PO stat, Atenolol 50mg PO stat, or Propranolol if thyroid-related) OR Calcium channel blocker OR Digoxin
Anticoagulation: Balance HAS-BLED risk vs CHA2DS2-VASc benefit. Start Clexane 1mg/kg SC stat in ED if no contraindications
Look for and treat underlying cause (THE ATRIAL FIBS mnemonic)
Recent evidence: No survival benefit in rate vs rhythm control. Rate control and anticoagulation supported by recent trials

Disposition: Internal Medicine referral. Admit for completion of NAC infusion.
Warnings: Toxic dose: 150 mg/kg or >10 g in adults; Use the Paracetamol Treatment Nomogram to establish need for antidote; If high dosage suspected, rather give the antidote; DO NOT withhold NAC even if ingestion was 48-72 hours prior to presentation; IF IN DOUBT, GIVE NAC; Start treatment preferably <8 hours post-ingestion; Paracetamol levels only meaningful if >4 hours post-ingestion; No need for AST/ALT/INR unless >12 hours post-ingestion; NAC cautions/side effects: Anaphylaxis, vomiting (less common with SNAP protocol); Separate IV infusion as may be inactivated by other medications; Paracetamol crosses placenta - administer NAC in pregnancy as above; High risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction, elderly; Staggered overdose: treat if total dose potentially toxic; Activated charcoal 50 g PO (not via NGT) if <1 hour from ingestion

reversal_agents.name: N-Acetylcysteine (NAC)
reversal_agents.dose: SNAP: 100 mg/kg over 2h then 200 mg/kg over 10h. Old: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h.

protocols.item: Calcium Channel Blocker and Beta-Blocker Overdose

cns: Profound CNS depression (beta-blocker OD may lead to this)

decontamination.activated_charcoal: Consider activated charcoal 50 g via NGT

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
Especially potassium
20 ml/kg if hypotensive
Amount and time of ingestion, co-ingestants, comorbidities, chronic medications, symptoms prior to arrival
Hypotension, bradycardia, hyperglycaemia (CCB) or hypoglycaemia (BB), acidosis, CNS depression
Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia. Note: Propranolol acts as Na channel blocker (TCA-like features). Sotalol blocks K channels (QT prolongation, Torsades).
Consider activated charcoal 50 g via NGT
Fluid bolus, high-dose inotropes may be required
Beta-blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression - prepare for airway management
50 ml 50% dextrose bolus + Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV stat. Start insulin infusion 0.5 IU/kg/hr, titrate up q30min to max 10 IU/kg/hr. Dextrose 25 g/hr via central line to maintain euglycaemia.
Calcium chloride 10 ml 10% over 10 min via large bore IV/central line. Target Ca 2 mmol/L.
For BB overdose - specialist consultation
Specialist consultation
If no response to HIET - specialist consultation
Renal consult - some beta-blockers dialyzable

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 50 ml 50% dextrose bolus + Actrapid 1 IU/kg (60-80 units) IV stat. Infusion: Actrapid 0.5 IU/kg/hr, titrate up every 30 min to max 10 IU/kg/hr. Dextrose: 25 g/hr maintenance (50 ml 50% dextrose/hr or 100 ml 25% dextrose) via central line.
drugs.frequency: Insulin titrated every 30 min. Dextrose continuous.
drugs.notes: Specific antidote for CCB and BB overdose. Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then every 1 hour. Monitor potassium - replace if <2.5 mmol/L.

drugs.adult_dose: 10 ml 10% over 10 minutes via large bore IV or central line
drugs.notes: For calcium channel blocker overdose specifically.
Consider for beta-blocker overdose. Specialist consultation required.
Consider for both CCB and BB overdose. Specialist consultation.

monitoring.frequency: Every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly

warnings: Propranolol: Also acts as Na channel blocker - TCA-like features of OD
Sotalol: Blocks myocardial K channels - QT prolongation, Torsades in OD
CCB causes hyperglycaemia; BB causes hypoglycaemia
HIET: Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly
Replace potassium if <2.5 mmol/L during HIET
Dextrose infusion via central line for euglycaemia maintenance
Therapeutic endpoints for HIET: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, urine output 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS
Consider ECMO if no response to HIET
Beta-blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression
Dihydropyridine class (non-selective CCBs) at lower doses may cause peripheral vasodilation with reflex tachycardia
protocols.disposition: Int Med consult (consider renal consult for dialysis). Will likely require ICU/High care. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
protocols.notes_updates:
Cns: CNS depression, Profound CNS depression (beta-blocker OD may lead to this)
Disposition: Int Med consult (consider renal consult for dialysis). Will likely require ICU/High care. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
Warnings: Propranolol: Also acts as Na channel blocker - TCA-like features of OD; Sotalol: Blocks myocardial K channels - QT prolongation, Torsades in OD; CCB causes hyperglycaemia; BB causes hypoglycaemia; HIET: Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly; Replace potassium if <2.5 mmol/L during HIET; Dextrose infusion via central line for euglycaemia maintenance; Therapeutic endpoints for HIET: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, urine output 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS; Consider ECMO if no response to HIET; Beta-blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression; Dihydropyridine class (non-selective CCBs) at lower doses may cause peripheral vasodilation with reflex tachycardia

reversal_agents.name: HIET (High-Dose Insulin)
reversal_agents.dose: Actrapid 1 IU/kg load then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr infusion

protocols.disposition: See admission_referral and discharge_criteria

glasgow_coma_scale.description: GCS scoring system for head injury assessment
glasgow_coma_scale.notes: Record best motor response. GCS < 13 on initial assessment OR < 15 at 2 hours after injury indicates need for CT.

ct_imaging_criteria.description: Indications for non-contrast CT Brain and C-spine in head injury
absolute_indications: GCS < 13 on initial assessment OR < 15 at 2 hours after injury on assessment in the ED
Suspected open/depressed skull fracture/base of skull fracture/facial fractures
Two or more episodes of vomiting since head injury

anticoagulant_patients.action: Perform non-contrast CT Brain and C-spine

relative_indications.description: If loss of consciousness or amnesia since head injury, check risk factors:
risk_factors: >30 minutes retrograde amnesia of event immediately before head injury

neuroprotective_measures.description: Measures to prevent secondary brain injury
neuroprotective_measures.o2_target: Normoxia (PaO2 60-100mmHg) - avoid hypoxia, O2 sats target > 95%
neuroprotective_measures.seizures: Monitor for seizures - Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV, max rate 50mg/min loading dose in traumatic ICH
neuroprotective_measures.sedation: Sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing/gagging - raises ICP
neuroprotective_measures.intubation_premed: Consider Fentanyl 1-3 mcg/kg over 60 seconds, 3 minutes prior to intubation
avoid: Neck pressure - C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV
Excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures

early_signs: Visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies (lateral gaze palsy)
late_signs: Cushing's triad: Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration
Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS

discharge_criteria: Responsible person available to accompany patient (if possible)
Head Injury Discharge Advice Sheet provided

warning_symptoms_return: Unconsciousness or lack of full consciousness
Drowsiness lasting > 1 hour when normally awake
Loss of balance or problems walking
Weakness in one or more arms or legs
Painful headache that won't go away
Bleeding from one or both ears
discharge_advice.expected_minor_symptoms: Mild headache, nausea (without vomiting), dizziness, irritability, concentration problems, memory problems, tiredness, lack of appetite, sleep problems - should resolve within 2 weeks
advice: DO NOT stay home alone for first 24 hours
Stay within easy reach of a telephone
Plenty of rest, avoid stressful situations
DO NOT take alcohol or drugs
DO NOT take sleeping pills, sedatives, or tranquilisers unless prescribed
DO NOT play contact sport for at least 3 weeks without doctor approval
DO NOT drive or operate machinery unless completely recovered

admission_referral.contact: Neurosurgery registrar on call at HJH during normal working hours; After 16:00 call CMJAH registrar
admission_referral.admission: Normal hours - HJH under Neurosurgery; After hours - HJH under General Surgery (Neurosurgery take-over next morning)
admission_criteria: Abnormal CT, no intervention - neuro observation/IV antibiotics/O2
Normal CT but not meeting discharge criteria

notes: EFAST cannot exclude Retroperitoneal or Hollow Viscus injuries
EFAST can ONLY confirm free fluid in the abdomen, haemo-pneumothorax, pericardial fluid
Less sensitive/specific in pregnancy and paediatrics

rush_protocol.note: RUSH protocol (Rapid Ultrasound for Shock and Hypotension) referenced as policy annexure. Standard views include: Heart (pericardial/subxiphoid), IVC, Lung (sliding/sign), Morrison's pouch/hepatorenal, Splenorenal, Suprapubic/pelvic, Paracolic gutters. Detailed protocol not available in extracted text.

protocols.disposition: {'low_risk': 'Non-specific pain < 6 weeks with no red flags, monoradiculopathy/sciatica - DC with simple analgesics and physio/spine OPD follow up', 'high_risk': 'Manage underlying cause, admit to appropriate subspecialty once stable'}
red_flags: New pain at extremes of age (< 20 or > 50)
Constant, non-mechanical pain, or worse lying down/at night
Immune suppression (HIV, DM, steroids, immune suppressants)
examination_concerning_features: Sensory: saddle anaesthesia, paraesthesia, sensory level
Reflexes: any abnormal reflexes, including Babinski
Sphincter dysfunction: decreased rectal tone, urinary retention/incontinence
Midline spinal tenderness, deformity, swelling, fluctuance
Systemic: wasting, fever, lymphadenopathy, other pathologic fractures

haematological: Spinal epidural haematoma - anticoagulants, recent lumbar puncture/epidural/spinal surgery

gynae: Lower back pain common in pregnancy

imaging.x_rays: Neither sensitive nor specific for back pain
imaging.mri: Back pain with new neurology, or suspicion for epidural abscess requires URGENT MRI

specific_management.epidural_abscess: Antibiotics - Consider Ceftriaxone, Cefazolin or Vancomycin

protocols.disposition: Certify death at time of BRAIN DEATH; Counsel family prior to cessation of mechanical ventilation; Consider for organ donation referral if appropriate
protocols.notes_updates: Disposition: Certify death at time of BRAIN DEATH; Counsel family prior to cessation of mechanical ventilation; Consider for organ donation referral if appropriate
prerequisites: Systolic blood pressure > 100mmHg (or MAP > 60, age appropriate paeds targets)
Significant metabolic, electrolyte, endocrine abnormality excluded
examination_criteria: Pupils fixed and non-reactive (do not have to be dilated)
Absent cough reflex on deep suctioning down ETT
No facial grimace to supra-orbital pain
Absence of motor response in all limbs (spinal reflexes may persist)
Absent oculovestibular (cold caloric) reflex - visible intact eardrum, HOB 30°, 50ml ice-cold water flushed into canal, observe for 60 seconds, eye movement should be absent

apnoea_test.performers: Two doctors should perform a single apnoea test together
apnoea_test.prerequisites: Only proceed if all other brainstem reflexes are absent and patient is haemodynamically stable (may be on inotropes)
steps: Disconnect from mechanical ventilation and supply continuous O2 via T-piece
No spontaneous respiratory effort once CO2 has risen > 60 and pH < 7.3 is compatible with brain death
Halt testing if patient becomes unstable or spontaneous respirations are witnessed

findings_incompatible_with_brain_death: True extensor or flexor motor response
protocols.requirements: Testing can be done by 2 doctors simultaneously, one of whom must have more than 5 years experience. No doctor may be involved with the transplant team.

protocols.disposition: Discuss with surgeon on call. Transfer to burns unit if indicated.

second_degree_partial.name: Partial thickness (superficial dermal)
second_degree_partial.appearance: Blisters, moist, very painful, pink/red, blanches

second_degree_deep.name: Deep partial thickness (deep dermal)
second_degree_deep.appearance: Moist/dry, mottled, less painful, does not blanch
second_degree_deep.healing: >21 days, scarring likely, may need grafting

third_degree.name: Full thickness
third_degree.appearance: Dry, leathery, charred, white/tan/brown, painless (nerve destruction)
third_degree.healing: Does not heal spontaneously, requires grafting

tbsa_calculation.method: Lund and Browder Chart or E-burn app
tbsa_calculation.notes: Most accurate method - accounts for age-related changes in body proportions. Rule of Nines can be used for rapid estimation in adults.

modified_brooke_formula.administration: Half the fluid in the first 8 hours, then half in the following 16 hours
modified_brooke_formula.notes: Smaller TBSA = normal maintenance fluids/oral intake UNLESS metabolic acidosis is present. See RMMCH Protocol book for detailed paeds burns fluid management.

management: Analgesia: Morphine 0.1mg/kg IVI or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI + Paracetamol
Dressings: Jelonet and gauze. Chloromex ointment for face, no dressing
Review at SOPD / Local Clinic
Discharge with appropriate analgesia
Analgesia: Paracetamol/NSAIDS + Morphine 0.1mg/kg or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI or Ketamine 1-2mg/kg IVI or 2-4mg/kg IMI
IV line and maintenance fluids
Venous blood gas
Catheter only for perineal/genital burns
Dressings: Jelonet and gauze. Chloromex ointment for face, no dressing
Discuss with surgeon on call

criteria: Partial thickness burns > 15% in adults; > 10% in paediatrics
management: Secure airway if needed
IV access and start fluid resuscitation according to Modified Brooke Formula
Remember maintenance fluid for children (see RMMCH protocols)
Urinary catheter and NGT for all patients, early NGT feeds
Analgesia: Paracetamol/NSAIDS + Morphine 0.1mg/kg or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI or Ketamine 1-2mg/kg IVI or Ketamine 2-4mg/kg IM. Consider analgesia + sedation infusion
Dressings: Jelonet and gauze. Chloromex ointment for face, no dressing
Discuss with surgeon on call ASAP

management_priorities: Burns < 3 hours: cool running water over the burn for 20 minutes
Dry the patient and keep warm
All blisters and debris must be removed - Adult: wash burn and surrounding area with sterile water/saline; Child: wash whole patient with sterile water/saline; shave head in child with facial burns
Evaluate depth and TBSA using Lund and Browder Chart or E-burn app
Call surgeon on call / transfer to burns unit (CHBAH Burns/CMJAH 163)
Consider TRAUMA and TOX as co-pathology

escharotomy.indications: Circumferential burn with vascular compromise - pain, paraesthesia, decreased pulse, pale limb
escharotomy.notes: Ideally by surgeon in theatre. Incisions along axial lines, medial and lateral of limbs and torso. 1-2cm through into normal tissues above and below the burn. Cut deep enough to view subcutaneous tissue - may need to breach fascia. Maintain haemostasis with pressure.
avoid_areas: Ulnar nerve - posterior to medial epicondyle
Common peroneal nerve - head of fibula
Posterior tibial artery and nerve - posterior to medial malleolus
escharotomy.hands: Cutaneous incisions strictly mid-axial lateral or medial to avoid palmar and digital neurovasculature. Never cut palmar aspects or tips of fingers.

suspect_if: Respiratory distress, stridor, accessory muscle use, new onset hoarseness
ABG: PaO2 < 60mmHg, CO > 10%, PF ratio < 200 mmHg
inhalation_injury.notes: Isolated nasal hair singeing/facial skin burns is NOT an indication for intubation
management: Intubate early, plan for difficult airway
LMA unlikely to be useful, plan for direct conversion to surgical airway in failed intubation
Ventilate with 100% O2 until CO toxicity is excluded
Consider inhaled bronchodilators, mucolytic agents (NAC), early chest physio, therapeutic bronchoscopy (ETT > 7.5), regular suctioning

suspect_if: Suicide attempt using vehicle exhaust fumes
Altered mental status, nausea, headache, confusion, ataxia, dizziness, seizures
O2 saturation gap: high sats, low PaO2 on ABG

electrical_injuries.household_low_voltage: 240V AC, superficial burns, rhabdomyolysis unlikely, cardiac dysrhythmias rare
electrical_injuries.industrial_high_voltage: >1000V up to 765kV, deep burns, tetany, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac dysrhythmias. Cable theft = high voltage
electrical_injuries.lightning: Up to 1 billion V DC, variable burns, ferning pattern/Lichtenberg figures (pathognomonic, fades 24-36hrs)
management: Monitor cardiac dysrhythmias - most resolve spontaneously
Monitor rhabdomyolysis, AKI, compartment syndrome
May need MORE fluids than TBSA estimation - titrate to UO > 2ml/kg/hr
Low voltage, no chest pain, no syncope, normal ECG: early DC
High voltage: cardiac monitoring 6-8 hrs

palliative_care.considerations: >50% TBSA - involve ED consultant and surgeon early, senior consultant decision ONLY
palliative_care.management: IV access for morphine and midazolam infusion, no investigations, comfort care, counsel family, offer psychology referral

burn_bundles_of_care.3_hours: Patient stabilised, fluid therapy commenced and monitored, analgesia, wounds cleaned and dressed, surgeons informed, escharotomy performed if needed

nexus_criteria.description: If ALL criteria met, NO indication for C-spine imaging

canadian_c_spine_rule.description: Stepwise decision tool for C-spine imaging
high_risk_factors: Focal peripheral neurological deficit or paraesthesias
Dangerous mechanism of injury: Pedestrian or cyclist struck by vehicle
Axial load to head e.g. diving
low_risk_factors: Comfortable in sitting position in ED
Ambulatory at any time since injury
canadian_c_spine_rule.range_of_motion_test: Able to rotate neck 45° left and right
canadian_c_spine_rule.ct_decision: If any indication for CT Brain as per head injury protocol: Perform non-contrast CT Brain AND C-spine
canadian_c_spine_rule.imaging: NO ROLE FOR X-RAYS IF CT AVAILABLE

warnings: TIME SENSITIVE LIMB THREATENING DIAGNOSIS! DO NOT DELAY!
protocols.notes_updates: Causes: Blunt soft tissue injury, Burns (especially electrical), Casts, Contrast extravasation/high-speed fluid injectors, DVT - phlegmasia cerulea/alba dolens, Fractures (common), Haemorrhage (Haemophilia), Infections, IV/IO infusions, Skin/skeletal traction, Snake bites (NB more likely PSEUDO-compartment syndrome)

six_ps.note: Classic signs - do NOT wait for all signs, especially late signs
early_signs: Pain out of proportion to the injury
late_signs: Pulselessness (pulses usually PRESENT until very late)
six_ps.notes: Absent pulses may be due to systemic hypotension, arterial occlusion or vascular injury. Do not delay until late signs appear.

pressure_thresholds.measurement: Serial or continuous measurements preferred. Can use A-line set.

management.urgency: TIME SENSITIVE LIMB THREATENING DIAGNOSIS! DO NOT DELAY!
steps: Do not delay surgery for imaging e.g. CT angio UNLESS truly necessary
Reduce fractures but avoid traction/tight casts
Place limb at the level of the heart
Liberal analgesia - avoid nerve blocks/regional anaesthesia!
Consider concomitant rhabdomyolysis and crush syndrome

causes: Snake bites (NB more likely PSEUDO-compartment syndrome)

general_principles: Reduce fractures (avoid traction/tight casts in compartment syndrome risk)
Regain length and alignment of involved bone

midshaft_femur.notes: Skin traction requires connection to bed with pulley system based on vectors of forces

coverage: Skin on medial aspect of lower leg
femoral_nerve_block.technique: Ultrasound guided, linear transducer in inguinal crease, femoral nerve lateral to common femoral artery, 20ml Marcaine (2-3mg/kg)

protocols.imaging: X-rays: joint above and below injury

primary_survey.name: CABC (ATLS adapted)
components: A - Airway with cervical spine protection
primary_survey.secondary_survey: DEFG - Detailed examination after primary survey stabilised

general_approach: Triage to RESUS AREA, even if patient initially appears well
Keep in headblocks until C-spine clear. No hard C-spine collars!
Remove from hard backboard ASAP to avoid pressure injuries
Fully undress and log-roll ALL potential polytrauma
2 large-bore IV lines (green, grey, orange), or IO/CVP if difficult access
Document head to toe clinical exam, log-roll findings, extremity pulses, neuro exam, compartment checks, core temp. Consider rectal/PV exam
Urine catheter, BHCG in all females of childbearing age
Warm and dry, monitor for hypothermia
Analgesia, ATT +/- antibiotics +/- Tranexamic acid if indicated
Minimum 1 blood gas, ideally serial gases to trend pH/lactate/Hb

Refractory shock: Consider spinal cord injury, occult bleeding, other causes e.g. anaphylaxis. Bind pelvis, bilateral finger thoracostomies, additional blood products, damage control surgery, indications for resuscitative thoracotomy
Discuss all polytrauma and ventilated cases with Surgery ASAP!
fluid_considerations: Avoid large crystalloid volumes - give blood products
Consider massive transfusion protocol: shock index > 1, major mechanism, patient factors, clinical gestalt
If a trauma patient requires adrenaline infusion, they most likely need more blood products/theatre. Exception: Spinal cord injury with neurogenic shock (consider phenylephrine infusion)
imaging: LODOX or CXR + Pelvis XR + extremities according to clinical findings
2 accredited EFASTs (cannot exclude retroperitoneal/hollow viscus, less sensitive in pregnancy + paeds)
Pan-scan: injuries to > 1 anatomical region, dangerous mechanism + decreased GCS, sensory fallout, unexplained rising lactate/dropping Hb/persistent haemodynamic instability
Head and Neck CT: see respective protocols. All trauma Head CT MUST include C-spine CT and facial views
NO Skull or C-spine X-rays in ED - CT is gold standard
Extremity X-rays: joint above and below injury
emergency_haemorrhage_control: Open wounds: Direct pressure, haemostatic sutures, catheter balloon tamponade, tourniquet
Fractures: Pelvic sheet binder, TRAC-3 splint, reduce and backslab
dangerous_mechanisms: MBA's unless extremely trivial e.g. stationary bike fell over
MVA's: high-velocity, unrestrained, roll-over, heavy vehicle collisions, ejection, T-bone, head-on, steering wheel impact
Assault: Mob, trunk GSW, multiple trunk stabs, screwdriver stabs

massive_transfusion.definition: Whole blood volume (~70ml/kg) in 24 hours, or 50% of blood volume in 4 hours
massive_transfusion.indications: Shock index > 1, major mechanism, patient factors, clinical gestalt

packed_red_cells.indications: Acute haemorrhage based on clinical features, lactate, vital signs, Hb < 10. Transfuse PRC and FFP 1:1 if > 1 unit PRC required
packed_red_cells.effect: 1 unit raises Hb by 1.5, Hct by 3%

fresh_frozen_plasma.indications: Acute haemorrhage, warfarin overdose with INR > 1.5 and active bleeding

platelets.indications: Plt <10 (consider even if not bleeding), Plt <50 with bleeding/invasive procedure, platelet dysfunction with active bleeding, massive transfusion protocol

freeze_dried_plasma.note: Ward stock, room temp. 1 unit FDP = 1 unit FFP. For life-threatening bleeding until FFP available.

protocols.disposition: Urgent Surgical consultation
protocols.notes_updates: Disposition: Urgent Surgical consultation

alvarado_score.description: Alvarado Score for diagnosis of acute appendicitis in adults

paediatric_appendicitis_score_pas.note: Patients in the low risk group do not have a zero risk. Treat according to clinical judgment.

management: ABCD approach to resuscitation
Hypovolaemia: 20 ml/kg Balsol/Ringers Lactate bolus
Septic shock: Fluids 20 ml/kg Balsol/Ringers Lactate bolus + Antibiotics: Augmentin 30mg/kg IV + Urgent Surgical consult
Analgesia: Morphine 0.1mg/kg
Antiemetic: Maxolon 0.1mg/kg (not in children < 1 year old)

investigations.radiological: Graded compression ultrasound/CT. CXR erect if perforation suspected
investigations.other: Tailor investigations to clinical findings and to rule in/rule out differential diagnoses

special_populations.paediatric: May present with more non-specific or atypical symptoms
special_populations.pregnancy: Due to displacement of uterus, presentation may be atypical. A leukocytosis in pregnancy is normal.

signs: Vital signs parallel degree of illness
Murphy's sign (inspiratory pause on RUQ palpation)

protocols.disposition: Urgent surgical referral
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: RUQ pain/epigastric pain, Nausea, Vomiting, Anorexia, Low grade fever
Signs: Vital signs parallel degree of illness, +/- Jaundice, Epigastric/RUQ tenderness, Abdominal guarding, Murphy's sign (inspiratory pause on RUQ palpation)
Risk factors: Increasing age, Female, Parity, Family history, Obesity, Diabetes Mellitus, Profound weight loss, Fasting, Cystic fibrosis, Malabsorption syndromes, Medications: antibiotics, lipid lowering, diuretics, oestrogen-containing, steroids
Disposition: Urgent surgical referral
risk_factors: Medications: antibiotics, lipid lowering, diuretics, oestrogen-containing, steroids

resuscitation: IV line - 20mls/kg Balsol/Ringer's Lactate fluid bolus if shocked

radiological: CXR erect, AXR supine and erect
CT Abdomen - not test of choice, only if diagnosis uncertain

ultrasound_features: Gallstones/sludge AND 1 or more of: GB wall thickening (>4mm), GB distention (diameter >4cm, length >10cm), Pericholecystic fluid, Dilated ducts (CBD or intrahepatic), Air in GB wall/lumen
specific_treatment: Analgesia - see Pain Management in ED Protocol

surgical_intervention: Urgent decompression for infected obstruction: ureteric stent or percutaneous nephrostomy
ESWL or ureteroscopy for stones not passing spontaneously

imaging.alternatives: Ultrasound (no radiation, good for pregnant patients, children, and when radiation should be avoided)
imaging.x_ray: KUB x-ray limited value, may show radiopaque stones

analgesia.ketorolac: 30-60mg IM/IV (potent NSAID, excellent for renal colic)
analgesia.morphine: 0.1mg/kg IV if NSAIDs contraindicated or insufficient
analgesia.caution: Avoid NSAIDs if AKI, dehydration, single kidney, pregnancy third trimester

medical_expulsive_therapy.tamsulosin: 0.4mg PO daily (alpha-blocker, relaxes ureteral smooth muscle)
medical_expulsive_therapy.notes: Controversial benefit for larger proximal stones

protocols.notes_updates:
Investigations - blood: FBC, Creatinine, HIV ELISA, RPR, Hepatitis B and C
Investigations - urine: Dipstix, Pregnancy test
examination: Physical examination (Male doctors = female chaperone present, vice versa for male patient)
Collect evidence with Sexual Assault Evidence Collection Kit (SAECK)

wound_management: Flush well with soap and water
Avoid suturing and local anaesthetic agents
Tetanus toxoid booster (TIG if severe wounds)

human_rabigam.administration: Infiltrate entire dose around/into wound. Dilute with saline if more volume needed for multiple wounds.
human_rabigam.timing: Can be given up to 7 days post vaccine administration, give ASAP

equine_equirab.note: 1:150,000 risk of anaphylaxis. Administer in setting with resuscitation equipment available.

classification.name: Uncomplicated Hyperglycaemia
classification.criteria: HGT >11 mmol/L, no ketones, no nausea/vomiting, no dehydration, normal mental state, no acidosis

classification.name: Complicated Hyperglycaemia (DKA/HHS)
classification.criteria: Any of: urine ketones positive, nausea/vomiting, dehydration, altered mental state, acidotic breathing, pH < 7.35, HCO3 < 20, BE < -4

monitoring.target: < 15 mmol/L before switching to dextrose-containing fluids

warnings: Always assess for DKA/HHS criteria in hyperglycaemic patients
protocols.disposition: Uncomplicated: discharge with referral to Poly Clinic/local clinic within 3 days. Complicated: Admit to Medicine.
protocols.notes_updates:
Causes: Infection (UTI, pneumonia), Infarction, Ischaemia, Ignorance (defaulter), Intoxication, Implantation (pregnancy), Intra-abdominal (pancreatitis, cholecystitis, appendicitis), Undiagnosed Type 1 DM (age < 35 years)
Diagnostic criteria:
  Blood glucose (HGT): > 11 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  pH: < 7.35  (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
  HCO3: < 20 mmol/L (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
  Base Excess: < -4  (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
Classification:
  Uncomplicated Hyperglycaemia: HGT >11 mmol/L, no ketones, no nausea/vomiting, no dehydration, normal mental state, no acidosis

{'reaction': 'Hypothermia', 'features': 'More common with massive transfusion of cooled products', 'management': 'Blood warming'}
{'reaction': 'Electrolyte abnormalities', 'features': 'Low Calcium/Magnesium/Potassium', 'management': 'Monitor and replace'}

interpretation.label: Severe
interpretation.action: Intubate, CT head immediately, neurosurgical referral

interpretation.label: Low risk
interpretation.action: C-spine imaging NOT indicated — clinical clearance possible

components.name: Dangerous mechanism (fall >1m, axial load, high-speed MVC, rollover, bicycle/ejected)
Delayed onset of neck pain (>12h after injury)?

interpretation.label: Imaging required
interpretation.action: C-spine CT/X-ray indicated — high-risk factors present

interpretation.label: Low risk
interpretation.action: Range of motion testing — if full ROM, no imaging needed

interpretation.label: No imaging
interpretation.action: C-spine imaging NOT indicated — clinically clear

interpretation.label: Unlikely appendicitis
interpretation.action: Observe — CT abdomen if clinical concern

interpretation.label: Possible appendicitis
interpretation.action: CT abdomen with contrast — surgical consult

components.name: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative)
Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilisation of lower extremity
Recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 4 weeks
Localised tenderness along deep venous system
Calf swelling >3cm compared to asymptomatic leg
Pitting oedema confined to symptomatic leg
Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT
Clinical signs of DVT (minimum 3 points leg swelling and pain with palpation of deep veins)
Alternative diagnosis less likely than PE
Immobilisation ≥3 days or surgery in previous 4 weeks

interpretation.label: PE unlikely
interpretation.action: PERC rule → D-dimer if PERC not met

interpretation.label: PE excluded
interpretation.action: No further testing needed — PE ruled out

interpretation.label: PERC not met
interpretation.action: Proceed to Wells score or D-dimer

components.name: Blood pressure: SBP <90 or DBP ≤60 mmHg

interpretation.label: Intermediate risk
interpretation.action: Consider short hospitalisation or outpatient — mortality 9%

interpretation.label: High risk
interpretation.action: Admit to hospital — severe pneumonia — mortality 15-40%

options.label: Present
options.desc: Infection, surgery, iodine load, OCP, etc.

interpretation.label: Total 24h fluid
interpretation.action: ½ in first 8h from time of burn, ½ in next 16h

interpretation.label: High risk of poor outcome
interpretation.action: Check full SOFA, start sepsis bundle, consider ICU

interpretation.label: TEWS Orange
interpretation.action: Senior review within 15 min — consider HDU

components.name: All other wounds (dirty, major, crush, burn, frostbite, devitalised tissue)

interpretation.label: No TIG needed
interpretation.action: Give Td booster if >10y since last dose

interpretation.label: TIG not needed
interpretation.action: Give Td booster if >5y since last dose

interpretation.label: Give Td + TIG
interpretation.action: Give Td + 250 IU TIG at separate site

components.name: Burns involving face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, or major joints
Circumferential burns of limbs or chest
Burns in patients with significant comorbidities
Any burn in a child <1 year

interpretation.label: Elevated
interpretation.action: Consider causes of high anion gap metabolic acidosis

interpretation.label: High
interpretation.action: MUDPILES — DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, etc.

interpretation.label: Corrected Na
interpretation.action: Use corrected Na for treatment decisions

interpretation.label: Free water deficit
interpretation.action: Replace slowly over 48-72h — max correction 10 mmol/L/day
